mechanism_narrative: A persistently low unemployment rate (UNRATE) signals a robust labor market and strong consumer confidence, which translates into stable demand for goods and services, ultimately supporting corporate earnings and driving positive S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: If UNRATE remains below 4% for 6 consecutive months, yet the S&P 500 produces negative 20-day returns in more than 40% of those periods, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: A significant and sustained increase in the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) indicates aggressive monetary tightening by the central bank, which raises the cost of capital for businesses and consumers, dampening economic activity and leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS increases by more than 0.5% over 3 months
failure_condition: If FEDFUNDS increases by more than 0.5% over 3 months, but the S&P 500 generates positive 60-day returns in over 50% of such instances, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: Robust retail sales (RSAFS) combined with a low unemployment rate (UNRATE) signify strong consumer spending power and confidence, which directly boosts corporate revenues and profits, leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Strong consumer spending trends in early 2019
failure_condition: If RSAFS shows positive year-over-year growth and UNRATE is below 4% for 6 consecutive months, yet the S&P 500's 20-day forward returns are negative on average, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: During periods of economic expansion, accelerating industrial production (INDPRO) growth indicates increasing manufacturing output and capacity utilization, signaling strong corporate health and driving positive S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: If INDPRO 3-month annualized growth exceeds 2% for two consecutive quarters within an expansionary regime, but the S&P 500's 60-day forward returns are negative in more than 35% of those periods, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp month-over-month decline in housing starts (HOUST) signals a significant contraction in the housing market and broader economic demand, leading to reduced investment and consumer confidence, which results in negative S&P 500 forward returns.
candidate_trigger: HOUST declines by more than 10% month-over-month
failure_condition: If HOUST declines by more than 10% month-over-month, but the S&P 500 produces positive 20-day returns in over 60% of such instances, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: Persistently low and stable consumer price inflation (CPIAUCSL) provides a predictable economic environment, reducing uncertainty for businesses and consumers, which encourages investment and spending, thereby supporting equity valuations and positive S&P 500 returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Low and stable inflation in early 2019
failure_condition: If CPIAUCSL year-over-year growth remains between 1.5% and 2.5% for 12 consecutive months, yet the S&P 500's 5-day forward returns are negative on average, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: Robust non-farm payroll employment growth (PAYEMS) coupled with strong real GDP growth (GDPC1) signifies a powerful and sustainable economic expansion, which translates into higher corporate revenues and profits, driving S&P 500 forward returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Solid job growth and GDP in 2018-2019
failure_condition: If PAYEMS shows positive month-over-month growth for 6 consecutive months and GDPC1 shows positive quarter-over-quarter growth for 2 consecutive quarters, but the S&P 500's 60-day forward returns are negative in more than 40% of those periods, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: When inflation (CPIAUCSL) is elevated and rising, any increase in the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) signals an aggressive monetary policy stance to cool the economy, which typically dampens corporate earnings expectations and leads to negative S&P 500 forward returns.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS increases by 0.25% in a month AND CPIAUCSL year-over-year growth > 3%
failure_condition: If FEDFUNDS increases by 0.25% in a month while CPIAUCSL year-over-year growth is above 3%, but the S&P 500 produces positive 20-day returns in over 55% of such instances, the hypothesis is rejected.
